title: virgil.org. http://virgil.org The Perseus Project provides Vergil's Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid in English and Latin and the Aeneid commentaries of Servius (Latin only) and Conington-Nettleship-Haverfield. Latin texts can be displayed without links to the Morpheus forms analyser, and in the case of the epic, to the commentaries as well, English texts with links to the Perseus look-up tool. The Vergil Project is officially under construction until Fall 2000, but it is functional already. It offers (1) on-line tools for disambiguation of grammar, analysis of syntax, translation, and entry of discursive commentary for individual words, phrases, or longer sections of text. Access to these tools is available upon request to qualified collaborators working individually or in groups; (2) Text and Commentary. The site currently offers a Latin text of the Aeneid based on the editions of Mynors and Geymonat, grammatical and syntactical analysis, and glossary of Aeneid 1-2; and a concordance. By December 1999 these resources will have been supplemented with grammatical and syntactical analysis and glossary of Aeneid 4 and 6, and discursive commentary on books 1, 2, 4, and 6. Virgil.org maintains a web site devoted to Vergil and Vergiliana. It is the home of "Mantovano", a threaded discussion list devoted to "the works attributed to Publius Vergilius Maro," "Commentary on Virgil and his works from late antiquity .. to the present," and "Virgil's influence on subsequent literature." The website contains a form through which one can register for "Mantovano" as well as sections on bibliography, vitae, Augustus, (Julius) Caesar, translations, links, books, maps, etc. Vergilian Bibliography 1998-

title: Vergils Tityrus und der perusinische Konflikt. Zum Verständnis der 1. Eclogue
publication_block: RhMus 141 (1998) 348-362. Harrison argues that Corydon is a "speaking name" (Gk. KopuBcóv "lark"). Ergo, "the Lark has been the lark for us", an acknowledgment of Corydon' s expert singing. Henderson weighs the main sectors of Eclogue 3, a mime about representation, a rumination on the spark of collaborative energy: Bickering (1-27), Bargaining (28-54), Balladeering (60-107), Balloting (55-59, 108-111), and Browsing: et quisquís (109). Papanghelis shows that Damoetas and Menalcas, from a Neoteric viewpoint, give contrasting but complementary aspects of the spirit and rhetoric of their literary circle. The exchanges of lines 84-91 are central and excite Palaemon's declaration of a draw. Literary self-consciousness is the ultimate test of the merits of the Vergilian herdsman-poet. Daphnis' judgment matches the fairness of Palaemon's: Corydon presented the better claim to superiority. Touratier examines different tenses in Eel. 7, 1-20. reviews: Soubiran, Latomus 57 (1998) 718-719. Schork detects three etymological allusions in the Georgics involving ancient Egyptian in a congenial wordplay: Georgics 4.208-121, 291; 3.28-29. Thomas regards the Georgics as a melding or integration of Hesiodic themes, maybe stylizations of agricultural folk elements, rustic Italian material, and new learned Alexandrian and Roman elements for modernity and new metaphorical meaning (213). The "rustic" or "archaic" elements undergo modernization, without loss of identity. "Songs of Hesiodic Greece, mediated through the refinement of Alexandrian Greece, would be appreciated by Vergil's audiences."

De Grummond' s thesis is that Vergil conceived images of the moon and the chase and of Diana as variants of single motif, mutable in its Vergilian Bibliography 1998-1999 95 repetitions, constituting a central theme in the epic. Coolness a reticence, withdrawal into one's self, flight from society, united a longing for companionship and fulfilment offered by hu fellowship are components of what De Grummond calls the "Di experience." The Camilla and Nisus and Euryalus episodes featur the discussion. Nugent focuses on the difference between male and fem suffering and moral choice in the epic. title: Ferae virtus : Anger in Virgil's Aeneid
publication_block: PASSIONS 169-184. Dangel's study treats prayers and epitaphs in Vergil and Catullus. Gill (no. 168) accents the contrast between the Aristotelian approach to the emotions and the Stoic, shared by the Hellenistic schools of philosophy, notably Epicureans. Roman poetry tends to present intense passion as a kind of madness, in Seneca and Vergil especially; passion is viewed as the outcome of akratic (weakwilled) self-surrender, a portrayal best explained as a fusion of Stoic psychology and Roman ethics. Gordon establishes Lucretius' DRN as a model in Aeneid 1 and 4, together with Homer's Odyssey and Apollonius Rhodius' Argonautica. Echoes of Nausicaa and other Phaeacian traditions reinforce Dido's link with the Garden. Disparagers of Epicurus called him the "Phaeacian philosopher." Finally, the inter-textual modes of the epic consign various possibilities of interpretation to the reader. The "Groupe" applies modern stylistic criticism to the characterization of Ajax in Ennius, Vergil, Lucan, Silius Italicus, and Homer. Helzle concentrates on the speeches of Dido, Turnus, and Aeneas. constructs a profile of Parthenius as a learned literary collaborato
with Cornelius Gallus, Cinna (?) and Vergil. He offered advice a
example as a scholar poet rather than schoolmaster. Jenkyn
explores ideas of nature throughout antiquity, but particular
Vergil's including landscape, history and patriotism in th
discussion. Jenkyns is concerned with the poetry and culture of th
first century BCE as a whole. Keith studies Sulpicia's six elegi
which regularly evoke the love story of Dido and Aenea
redeploying the diction and recombining Vergilian themes a
imagery. Sulpicia's multifaceted, thoroughly distinctive fema
persona enables the her to invoke a complex of erotic desire
sensations, fears and satisfactions. "Sulpicia's poetry compels o
recognition of tensions among competing ideological positions in t
construction of sexuality and gender in Augustan Rome - too rar
glimpsed in the male-authored literature of her contemporarie

mythographer provides a translation, commentary, and contextu

stories. Vergil's Daedalus reverberates in Pavlock's article on h

title: Repetition in Latin Poetry: Figures of Allusion
reviews: Nelis, BMCR 9 (1998) 685-687; O'Hara, JRS 88 (1998) Vergilian Bibliography 1998-1999 103 197; Vine, CJ 94 (1998-1999) 195-200; Zetzel, CP 94 (1999 103-111. Adams and Mayer review the contents of the Proceedings of the British Academy volume 93 (1999) devoted to "Aspects of the Language of Latin Poetry" under separate rubrics: Neutral Terms, Prosaic Terms, Formal Speech or Prose, Colloquialism and Orality, Archaism, The Influence of Greek, Metaphor, and Word Order; Hyperbaton. Adams deals with the relationship between ordinary usage and certain "poetic" patterns. Coleman surveys the concepts of poetic diction, discourse, and register, including archaism and the debt to Greek, including the use of metaphor and specifically poetic syntax. Görler' s article bears on unexpected usages of transitive verbs which may amount to metaphor. Langslow deals with scientific language, Mayer with the influence of Greek and the use of technical vocabularies. Novara is concerned with tibicines: Aeneid 1.534, 7.258, 1.560. Penney 's focus is on syntactic features that derive from the oldest stratum of Latin usage

title: Divided Allusion: Virgil and the Coma Berenices
publication_block: HSCP 98 (1998) 277-305. Balot refines and extends Wilkinson's Pindaric reading of the proem with specific, neglected parallels betwen Vergil and Octavian. Putnam analyzes Aeneid 12.896-900 for allusions, divine manipulation and the nature of heroism in the interplay of Homer and Vergil. "Aeneas' enraged slaying of his suppliant antagonist... runs blatantly counter to his father's ennobling dictum to spare the humbled foe (and) does not augur well for a sustained future, in his hands, of heroism as we know it from Homer and as Anchises movingly commands its moderation for Rome to come" (78). Wills studies Catullus 66.39 and Vergil, Aeneid 6.456 and questions why the imitation is so close, and seeks to determine the boundaries of the allusion

title: Ripae ulterioris amore: Structure and Desire in Aeneid 6
publication_block: CJ95 (2000) 349-361. Smith argues that Vergil shaped the Sinon-Laocoon episode to be a story of the systematic perversion of religious sacrifice that turns inevitably on the perversion of human knowledge. Homer generally treated the last two feet of the epic verse as an unbroken rhythmical unit. Pontes shows that the Aeneid resorts frequently to sensebreaks, attested by ancient punctuation, within the heroic clausula. He considers the rhetorical and poetic effect of the 'broken' heroic clausula in Aeneid 2 and 3.. Casali tracks ambiguity and complexity in Dido's address to Aeneas {Aen. 4.416-36) and explores the implications of her request for tempus inane : did Aeneas make a mistake? Warden supplies a detailed discussion of correspondences 140 Alexander G. McKay and contrasts in passages that precede the cr relate to the crossing and what lies beyond

Gomez Pallares links the basic meaning of latro (Aen. 12.1-8) (an armed man who stalks and wounds his victim) with the popular Roman board game (ludus latrunculorum ) where military strategy was basic. Hahn studies Vergil's transformation of an Homeric ritual, specifically associated with a treaty oath, into an ambiguous ritual more reminiscent of an ordinary sacrifice. Hahn follows Servius who detected a Roman preparatory measure rather than Greek sacrificial practice in Aen. 12.173-74. Vergilian Bibliography, 1999-2000 141

title: Remembering the enemy: narrative, focalization, and Vergil's portrait of Achilles
publication_block: TAPA 129 (1999) 225-261. Fantham's study of Turnus' role in the council debate ( Aeneid 11) and in the battle episodes from his triumph in Aeneid 10 to his death, sets Turnus in a more compassionate light; Vergil's depiction of the resistance hero's weakness, and his absorption with an egocentric quest for glory, nevertheless retains our pity for the defeated hero. Papaioannou demonstrates that Vergil transformed the mentality and behaviour of Diomedes in Homeric epic and incorporated his transformation in the new intellectual milieu of the Augustan age. Diomedes has no enemy, and no rival in Patavium; his destiny foreshadows Aeneas' cultural integration. Smith accents Vergil's use of Achilles as nuanced figure, central to an understanding of the epic poem and its hero. Achilles, on the testimony of Aeneas, Priam,, and Neptune, emerges not simply as a symbol of violence but as a more complex figure embodying wrath and pietas. Just as Achilles shares Aeneas' pietas, so Aeneas shares Achilles' destructive nature. 142 Alexander G. McKay

behaviour of Diomedes in Homeric epic and incorporated his
transformation in the new intellectual milieu of the Augustan age.
Diomedes has no enemy, and no rival in Patavium; his destiny
foreshadows Aeneas' cultural integration. Smith accents Vergil's use
of Achilles as nuanced figure, central to an understanding of the epic
poem and its hero. Achilles, on the testimony of Aeneas, Priam,, and
Neptune, emerges not simply as a symbol of violence but as a more
complex figure embodying wrath and pietas. Just as Achilles shares
Aeneas' pietas, so Aeneas shares Achilles' destructive nature.
142 Alexander G. McKay

Paul Fowler leaves his collected articles as legacy; they are abiding
memorials of a learned, provocative scholar and mentor. Gladiatorial
imagery, which surfaces in the combat scenes, was familiar to all
ranks of Roman society. Pinkster analyses Vergil's use of the
present tense for narrative events in the epic, while retaining traces
of the features familiar in oral story telling. "Difficulties in explaining
the use of the present disappear, when we adopt a broader view of
the functions the present fulfils in the Latin tense system." Thomas'
collection of his articles and notes, published between 1979 and
1998, treats the poet's transformation and renovation of his
intertextual models, with emphasis on Callimachus. Thomas'
response to R. K. Balot's article "Pindar, Virgil, and the Proem to
Geòrgie 3," Phoenix 52 (1998) 83-94 occupies pages 9-10, footnote
13.
Vergilian Bibliography, 1999-2000 147

publication_block: Philologus 144 (2000) 90-115. Clark argues for the likelihood that Vergil took Aeacus' speech (Vat. gr. 2228, f. 482) and Euripidean function as model for Charon's speech ( Aen . 6.388-97) and created an innovative psychological dynamic for Charon. Faber brings the pseudo-Hesiodic Shield of Heracles into conjunction with Aeneas' shield. The allusions recall dreadful imagery unique to the shield of Heracles and so intensify the destructive power of Aeneas' armor, for not only the shield, but greaves, breastplate and sword are portrayed in language borrowed from Heracles' shield. 148 Alexander G. title: Seamus Heaney's Exposure and Vergil's Aeneid
publication_block: CML 19 (1999) 243-256. Todd's article sheds new light on the context of Enoch Powell's immigration speech in Birmingham, England in April 1968, "one of the most notorious usages of a classical quotation in 20th century British political life." Todd augments the observations of H. H. Huxley in Vergilius 44 (1998) 24-27. Williams studies the forty lines of Heaney's poem "Exposure" for reflections of essential elements of the Aeneid. Unlike Aeneas, the hero of Heaney's poem cannot see and recognize his comet, and so is unable to fulfill his destiny. 154 Alexander G. McKay

title: Horatian Landscape in the Louvre's 'Concert Champêtre'?
publication_block: Artibus et Historiae 41 (2000) 123- 131. Finley discusses and reproduces eleven pictures by Turner that are "either directly or indirectly concerned with the hero, Aeneas, and his exploits." Heuze studies Vergil "portraits" in the Sousse (Hadrumetum) mosaic and in Signorelli's "Dantesque" portrait in the marble intarsia flooring of the Duomo of Siena. Kilpatrick counters Christiane Joost-Gaugier' s 1999 interpretion of the Giorgione-Titian painting as evocative of Vergil's Arcadian melancholy. He detects Propertius in the elegant lute-player in red and Horace in the rustic figure gazed upon by Euterpe, with the Sabine farm in the distance

Mazzocchini's analysis of the war narrative in the epic, with the lists of the dead, is thorough and provocative. Perutelli's "profile" of Eduard Norden' s impressive scholarship on the epic portrays the savant in his contemporary world. Ricottilli' s study of gesture and word expands Highet's magisterial study of the speeches in Vergil. Ernst Schmidt's articles, particularly on "American and German Approaches," a veritable "battlefield" of contrasting approaches and views, strains to accent the humanitas and comprehensive nature of Vergil's Weltgedicht. "He wrote the Aeneid to bring to light fundamental questions of humanity, foremost among them the relation and antagonism of state and empire, that is, power and historical mission, on the one hand; and on the other, human happiness, that is, a life free of sacrifice, suffering, and guilt" (171). title: Narrative Structure and Aeneid: The Frame of Book 6
reviews: Borgo, BStudLat 30 (2000) 715-716; Nishimura-Jensen, Vergilius 46 (2000) 175-180. Adkin accents accedo (Aen. 1 . 1 95-202), with its suggestion of "attachment and alliance," and accersit at Aen. 5.746, implying "assistance," as another instance of Vergil's etymologizing craft. Brucia argues that Harpalyce, who surfaces in the Venus episode at Carthage, ominously conflates the essence of two women: a fast-tracking warrior princess, and a rape-victim, a child-killer metamorphosed into a bird. Celaeno, the Harpy, provides another awesome party, and a measure of word-play in the Strophades. Abbott is concerned with Coroebus' stratagem (2.370-434) and his defence of "just deceit." In fact, the recurring nexus of dolus, arma, and civil strife (furor) reveals that dolus is malus. Anderson and Davis shed light on the vexed tradition of the Wooden Horse: both articles are noteworthy for their learning and innovative approach. Scafoglio reviews the controversial Helen episode and argues for ultrici flamma at 587 as preferable to ultricis flammae. Clement's article highlights the Breughelesque that surfaces in Vergil's epic. Konstan organizes Catullus (66.39-41), A en. 6.460, Callimachus, Sophocles' Antigone , and testor/testa, to resolve the pun. Smith suggests that Nero's Decursio sestertii (ca. A.D. 62-63) depict a Lusus Troiae squadron reminiscent of Aen. 5.545-605. Bleisch brings sympathetic Vergilian Bibliography 2000-2001 203 insights to the Deiphobus figure and Aeneas' encounter with him Hades. title: L'utopia e la storia. Il libro xu dell' Eneide e antologia delle opere. Torino
reviews: Landolfi, Lexis 17 (1999) 398-404. The ecphrasis of the Shield of Aeneas is a perennial topic. Scully's approach is novel: he detects destructive elements of fire and passion transmuted from savagery to victory flames and images. But, the conversion is incomplete, because traces of base metal remain. Fowler and Fucecchi shed complementary light on the Nisus and Euryalus episode in the ninth book. Fowler also treats the significance of the depiction on Pallas' baldric (Aen. 10.495-99 and 12.940-52) under "The Ruin of Time: Monuments and Survival at Rome," in his Roman Constructions (see item 204 below). Jacobson suggests that the proper name Ia-pyx, when aligned with Greek pyxis "medicine box," befits the healer and his drugs. According to Beare, Vergil's common swallow (Juturna's counterpart at Aen. 12.473-78) gathered up flies, small edibles, suitable for nestlings. Casali shows that Idmon, Turnus' messenger to Aeneas on the eve of the duel, is indebted to the prophet who participates in the Argonauti expedition, certain of death. So Vergil suggests that Turnus' recourse to Idmon signals his awareness of the same inevitability. Nicoli perceives that the near disaster for Aeneas, wounded on the field, and its reversal, signals the necessity of Turnus' death and guarantees Aeneas' progress towards apotheosis (12.793-94). Toptsi accents the penitential and retributive quality of Turnus' death, and the poet's concern with the preventive/providential factor. "It is an act of restraint planned ultimately by Jupiter and executed by the hand of Aeneas, although ignorant of the cosmic significance of his Vergilian Bibliography 2000-2001 205 act, which aims at securing the stability of the social order Aen struggles to establish in Italy" (177)

title: A. M. Keith, Engendering Rome: Women in Latin Epic. Roman Literature and Its Contexts
reviews: Gale, G&R 48 (2001) 95; Damien Neliš, Vergilius 46 (2000) 192-196. Binder's study of the reception of the Dido-tragedy is comprehensive and directional. Burden's anthology of essays contains a welcome account of Elizabethan Dido (Purkiss) a dazzling gallery attesting to Dido's presence in the visual arts (Montagu), a fresh interpretation of Mercury's double visit (Feeney), and a search for metatheatrical elements in Dido's tragedy (Pobjoy). Drake's essay points out that the connection with the Punic Wars in book 4 is pervasive and that Vergil's sympathetic portrayal of Dido is part of a complex reading of Rome's rise to power and a demonstration that the struggle with Carthage was woven into the same fate by which Romans had been destined to rule the world. Drake argues that book 4 overall is an allegory of the three Punic Wars, and a pessimistic interpretation of recent events; the fall of the Republic and the destruction of Carthage were interconnected

title: Vergil's Aeneid. Augustan Epic and Political Context
reviews: Novara, REL 11 (1999) 341-343; Schmitzer, Gymnasium 107 (2000) 542-544. Critics have returned to terra firma in their review of Roman monuments and their application to Vergil's texts. Diane Favro's study of The Urban Image has excited lynx-eyed scrutiny of Augustan Rome by students and scholars, and has evoked some illuminating reviews of her "trial" venture. Habinek's Politics and (with Schiesaro) Cultural Revolution break neglected ground and offer provocative views for Vergi lians to ponder. title: Repetition in Latin Poetry. Figures of Allusion
reviews: Dangel, Latomus 59 (2000) 149-152. Vergilian metrics have regained critical attention as support for character and context. Farrell's study of Latin Language and Latin Culture offers a different wide-ranging view of Latin as a language and as a vehicle for cultural practice: Vergilians should welcome this important examination of Vergil's medium. Laird's study of speech presentation offers valuable testimony and acute criticism, and can be profitably associated with Lobe's perceptive study. Putnam's study of the nature and role of ekphraseis in the epic offers a summary challenge to past and future understanding of their implications for the epic. Richard Thomas' essays on intertextuality in Vergil offer instructive and cogent "new" directions for Vergilian scholars. Wills continues to excite appreciative critical response to his cogent study of repetition and figures of allusion

title: Patria Praecepta : Lucretius and Vergil in the Underworld
publication_block: Vergilius 46 (2000) 83-92. Breed targets echo ( resonare ) as a phenomenon of the fictional world, and as metaphor for relations between texts. Vergil's Bucolics are traditional, but echo allows him to sidestep literary chronology. "The Bucolics can be read alongside, not strictly after, his modern texts." Gale's exploration of Vergil's indebtedness to resonances and instruction in Lucretius' didactic poem is welcome and authoritative. Giesecke' s study of cross-generic imitation of Lucretius' poem treats a formative factor in Vergil's art and complements Gale's work. Warden's study of patria praecepta examines the "dialogue" involving Aen. 6.719-853 and DRN 3.1024-1094 where Vergil disputes with (and overturns) Lucretius on many counts

title: Vergil Reading His Twentieth-Century Readers: A Study of Eclogue 9
publication_block: Vergilius 47 (2001) 64-88. The "competitive" aspect of Bucolic songs, Theocritus through Vergil to the Middle Ages remains constant. Baumbach accents two different approaches to the same topic in the Fifth Bucolic, and two concepts of poetry, oral and written, with a discussion of inspiration and imitation, and the reader as judge of their merits. Breed studies song and voice in the Sixth Bucolic as an ecphrastic subject. The centerpiece (31-86) is not a dramatization but a narration by the primary narrator, Tityrus. Paschalis re-awakens interest in the interplay of science and myth in Silenus' song in the same poem. Perkell surveys the late 20th-century critical landscape (with an appended bibliography, 86-88) and finds that Eclogue 9 typifies a dichotomy of interpretive opinion divided between "optimistic" and "pessimistic" parties. The division "seems to be a function of parallel deployment of describable interpretive conventions, corresponding to observed structural features of the text." (85). Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 135

title: Epic Lessons. An Introduction to Ancient Didactic Poetry
reviews: Parroni, Gnomon 74 (2002) 440-42. Cramer's analysis and provocative discussion continues to challenge the 'Harvard School.' proponents of disillusionment and contrary political sentiments. Monica Gale's work calls for attentive reading and speculation on didactic poetry. Gowers is concerned with kitchen gardens and their produce in Vergil and Columella, a seductive genre which Vergil felt obliged to omit while extolling the aged Tarentine gardener. Thomas shows that English and 'colonial' responses to the Georgics lack uniformity. He scrutinizes twentiethcentury translations and adaptations of the poems, and adduces literal and cultural intertexts extending from the distant period of the English civil war to recent Irish poets, Kavanaugh and Heaney, measuring 'resistance' poets against readings of the Georgics as idealized country life

title: Die Traumdarstellungen in der griechischrömischen Dichtung
publication_block: Munich and Leipzig. 2001. Armstrong studies the symbolic function of Crete in its repeated instances as a reminder of the temptations and inevitability of regression, repetition, and diversion from the linear course of history. Crete is portrayed as a cradle of deceit and monstrous passions, and of plague; but it is also a source of cures. Dreams, as extensions of poetic vision, images of prophecy and human psychology, and insanity are much in vogue for several scholars (Bouquet, Hershkowitz, Lake, Scafoglio, and Walde) and their studies enrich our understanding and appreciation of the poet's genius. Julia Dyson's study of Diana Nemorensis accents her importance as agent and cultic backdrop to sacrificial aspects of the epic. Lowrie's contribution to the University of Pennsylvania Conference (#5) argues for 'seriously playful' readings of Vergil's works as antidote to political and aesthetic concentration on the stark contrasts between good and evil. Markus studies Vergilian 'performance' in the context of his age (pp. 168-71). Battle narratives and casualty lists preoccupy Mazzochini. His chief concern is with poetic composition and narrative skill; his line by line commentaries on each selection is highly instructive. Most's contribution to "The Vergilian Century" (#5) examines Vergil's complete repertoire to develop new insights into memory's role in its poetic, erotic, didactic, and political dimensions. The relationship of remembering and forgetting emerges as central to major issues in Vergilian criticism. Aeneas' final act is not rooted exclusively in anger but is a gesture fuelled by memory and forgetfulness. Newman's brief essay explores neglected aspects of the Etruscan content of the epic and Vergil's possible Etruscan lineage, a favorable factor in his relationship with Etruscan Maecenas. Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 1 39 Oliensis examines the unexplored relationship between Vergil's ep and its place as Freudian dream work. Bloom, Hardie, and Reckfor pass in review with other neglected experimental readings of the 6 and 70s, and recent gender-oriented readings. Performance an gesture are treated from opposite poles by Campbell and Ricottill Shiffman's Platonic reading of the epic is fresh and illuminating. AENEID: INDIVIDUAL BOOKS l-VI

title: Vergil's Daedalus
publication_block: CO 79 (2002) 137-43. Khan's meticulous "appreciation" of Amor at the banquet of Aeneid 1 embodies sophisticated responses to Vergil's presentation of the process through which Dido succumbs to love through the agency of Amor. Five passages of crucial importance are involved: 1.657-60; 673-75; 683-88; 707-14; and 717-22. The analysis and discussion throughout is sensible and sensitive. Tragedy (particularly Aeschylean) and epic are brought into a single focus by several scholars (Krummen, Laigneau, and Scafoglio). Research into the tragic factor in Vergil's epic has become something of a preoccupation of British and North American scholarship and important studies on a major scale are in gestation. Book 5, a perennial favorite of students who are devotees of boating and boxing and contests of a wider variety, gains fresh attention for its larger political implications and its paradigms. Khan, in his study of Aeneid 2.780-84, and 3.1-8, stresses Creusa' s valedictory as prophecy incorporating the fatality and kingdom motifs exploited later in Apollo's oracle, in Anchises' visionary offering, and elsewhere. After arguing that Vergil modelled Charon's challenge to Aeneas in Aeneid 6.388-97 on Aeacus' challenge to Heracles in an extant fragment of Pirithous, composed by Euripides or Critias [CQ 50 (2000) 192-96], Clark now addresses Vergil's re-arrangement and expansion of the Underworld prior to the crossing of the Styx. Aeneas passes through more traditional experiences and horror earlier in his Catabasis than any of his prototypes, Heracles, Dionysus, or Orpheus. Jacobson reads Aeneid 6.45-48 as a question complementing the interrogation Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 141 in lines 42ff. title: Vergil on Killing Parthenius (A en. 10.748)
publication_block: CJ 97 (2001) 51-54. HorsfalPs somewhat daunting but immensely rich commentary on the Seventh Aeneid continues to excite admiration, respectful queries, and alternative readings. Miller's examination of the Palatine Temple of Apollo as the unexpected goal for Octavian' s victory parade in 23 B.C.E., duly reflected in the central circle of Aeneas' shield, was also favored by Nero in December a.D. 67. Quint's impressive article argues that the memory of Sarpedon, introduced first at Aeneid 1.94-101, with Hector, "dictated the logic of Virgil's imitations of Homer in Book 10." Quint's article submits Book 10 to a searching and skilful analysis and discussion. His concerns are with (1) Nymphs, Ships, and Pines; (2) The Brothers of Sarpedon; (3) The Sequence of Battle in Aeneid 10, concentrating on the principal actors - Pallas, Aeneas, Turnus, Mezentius and Orodes, Mezentius, Lausus, and Aeneas, and their debt to Homeric paradigms; and (4) A Question of Survival. Finally, "Like Pallas, all the Sarpedon-like victims of the Aeneid are dying - in the historical long run - for 1 42 Alexander G . McKay the sake of lulus and a specifically Augustan future lege. title: Pallas/ Athena in and out of the Aeneid
publication_block: In Athena in the Classical World. Susan Deacy and Alexandra Villing, eds. Leiden, Boston, Köln. 2001. 331-47. Vergilian Bibliography 2001-2002 143 Sarpedon draws the limelight in Aeneid 10 but the cast is also impressive elsewhere. Bateman properly detects Cleopatra and Anton as models for the Punic romance. Christoph parades the Horatii wit Aeneas as heroic examples. Cucchiarelli finds that Ovid's depiction of Orpheus is a mixture of Vergil's Orpheus and of Gallus ( Eclogu 10). Their destinies were closely comparable. Henderson's bree article speculates on the particular case which the Aeneid holds up for our collusion in handling the downside of the creation of a soc memory for Roman Italy. Ovid, Vergil, and Livy, and Ardea, myth cal and actual, are brought into unexpected and mutually informa tive focus; Servius supplies a surprising amen after a recital of su pression and horror at holy Ardea: totius autem Italiae curiosissimum fuisse Vergiliům multifariam apparet. Henderson's parting shot is unsettling and memorable: "The Aeneid winds up - Ovid saw it could run this way - trying harder than ever to make us remember all the forgetting" (p. 23). Read this and wonder. Hughes studies connecting theme and variations involving Creusa and Andromache. Keith's admirably compact volume gains strength in the parade of perceptive reviewers. Panoussi provides a remarkably sensitive perception of Vergil's Ajax from the standpoint of allusion, tragic and heroic identity. Her study joins forces with other scholars who have been recently sensitive to the tragic force of the epic. Perotti' s examination of the virtues and faults of Euryalus and Nisus, particularly in the night exploit, is concise and persuasive. Rivoltella' s essay focuses on the plaintive pursuit of the deceased (Creusa' s retention and elevation is equated with death) by the abandoned (or deceived) male party. Spence's review of the appearances of Athena/Minerva inside and outside the epic action, even by suggestion in the final compact on Olympus, is welcome and provocative

title: Virgile, Auguste et Apollon: mythes e politique à Rome: L 'Arc et la Lyre
reviews: Brisson Latomus 60 (1999) 1016-17. 144 Alexander G. McKay Delcourt's review of the role of Evander in the Pallanteum-Rome legend deals effectively with several interpretations of the legend, especially with Vergil, Aeneid 8.844-49. Evander earns respect as a wise king and civilizer, as a veritable humanist before literature itself. His Italian Arcady abides because it embodies the ideal life of humility and proximity to nature. Lest we forget, Dingel' s article highlights Vergil's elevation of the eponym of the Gens Iulia to the stature of king by identifying lulus with Ascanius, but does not make him forefather of the Kings at Alba Longa. Jupiter confirms and endorses the derivation of lulus from Ilus {Aeneid 1 .267 ff.) and so underscores Trojan ancestry

Callatay revels in the polysemy of the Vitruvian text, and constructs

an untenable friendship involving Vergil and the architect, fortu
ambo, where Nisus equates with Vitruvius and Euryalus with Ve
lodged together in the circle of Maecenas. Ogden's article include
Avernus in Campania is his brief catalogue of Nekyomanteia,
unfortunately discards necromancy in Italian caves (which aboun
in the Campi Phlegraei) for simpler lakeside precincts and incub
tion. Temple's study revives Robert Paget's observations and arg
ments in favor of an Antrum associated with the Mysteries (Orph
Eleusinian?). He provides an exciting account of personal desc
(and returns) with memorable photographs as evidence of renew
exploration and strives to rouse new awareness of the nature
complexity of the Baian tunnel. Rehak's careful study of the
Pacis relief of the sacrifice of the brood sow (endorsed by Verg
Varro, and Dionysius of Halicarnassus) favors the second ki
Numa Pompilius, over Aeneas, as the "ideal of the peaceful ru

title: Virgil and the Augustan Rec
reviews: Hardie, TLS 15 June 2001, 32; Hill, G&R 49 (2002) 1 14; Rea, CO 79 (2002) 161; McGill, BMCR 2001. 08. 23. Putnam's contribution to the University of Pennsylvania Conference (#5) detects a distillation of Vergilian themes, ideas, and motifs in Ovid's Myrrha episode, and employs intertextual relationships to explicate the transformation of Vergilian into Ovidian poetry. Ovid's intertextual exploration of Vergilian ideas and motifs sheds light on Aeneas who undergoes 'metamorphosis' into Pallas; he argues that a series of Vergilian transformations through exile and death anticipate the Metamorphoses, Tristia, and Epistulae ex Ponto

Beck-Chauvard studies the grieving mother of Euryalus ( Aen . 9.481-3) and Dido as Vergil's female community in grief, comparable to paternal sorrows of Mezentius and Evander. Boyd's revision of Pharr's monumental school text includes updates and additions to provide support for Advanced Placement classes. Clausen's study of decorum, allusion and ideology, is a greatly revised version of his Berkeley Sather Lecture (1987), entitled Vergil's Aeneid and the Tradition of Hellenistic Poetry. The La Fleur/McKay Upper Level student book (hardcover) includes Advanced Placement readings and meets requirements of the International Baccalaureate Latin syllabus. A substantial Teacher's Guide contains extensive discussion and teaching tips, literal translation of all selections, a bibliography and resource list. Galinsky's perceptive comments on drama in the epic are instructive, insightful and valid. Perkell's important article studies contradictions as a recurrent feature of Vergil's work and recognizes interpretive challenges faced by scholars and others. The text offers no resolution of the contradictions in Vergil's presentation of the Golden Age; they abide as Vergil's acknowledgment of a complex world replete with uncertainties. Putnam's article deals with ways to assess the epic and is both direct and familiar. 122 Alexander G. McKay AENEID: INDIVIDUAL BOOKS l-VI

Chew's remarks on ignorance, uncertainty and inconsistency Aeneas' nature are helpful. Hughes examines the structure and char terization of Hector's return to Troy and his meeting with Androma (Iliad 6) to help shape our understanding of the relationship betwee Aeneas and Dido. Merriam sees the appearances of Ascanius as barometer of storms and turmoil and near disasters. Alden Smith imparts a "modern" touch to Dido's "performance" in the guise of a chanteuse, a British diva whose stanzas relate the loss of her "cowboy" in "My Lover's Gone" ["No Angel" album, Arista Records 1999]. Starr shows how experience with rhetoric and oratory enriches Vergil's presentation of Aeneas and his dilemma at Aeneid 4.279-285. The Bacchic associations of Amata, another tragic queen, as frenzied Bacchant, and Aeneas' dual nature, both Apolline and Dionysian, offer readers and critics important new avenues for reflection on the ambiguity and characterization of two memorable performers. 126 Alexander G

Adler traces Vergil's treatment of Epicureanism in Vergil's epic, an
notices that "as the center of the de Rerum Natura had been
Lucretius' denial of Acheron, the center of the Aeneid is Vergil's
assertion of Acheron. The Aeneid is Vergil's battle against Lucretius
on behalf of men's belief in divine anger and punishment" (225-226).
Ando dwells on Vergil's concept of Italia and its overall conceptual
history. Monica Gale explores the implications of parallels between
three Vergilian episodes involving the hero's backward glance too
soon and too late. Parallels and contrasts between the episodes
highlight Vergil's exploration of dichotomies between poetry and
politics, individual and community, past and future. Brant Hannah

Gross focuses on Pallas' funeral rites and Pallas as immolation, and offers an explanation of Aeneas' controversial statement at the close. "By killing Turnus, Aeneas in a very real sense annihilates himself (and his past). Turnus is Aeneas' alter ego." Bleisch accents the crucial significance of the Regia passage for the epic. Marinčič relates the Arcadia theme of Eclogue 4 to the historical myth of Hercules and Cacus. Galinsky interprets the end of the Aeneid as Vergil's reworking of Roman historical epic in response to themes of Greek tragic drama

title: Die Ovid- und Vergil- Revision in tiberischer Zeit. Band I. Prolegomena
reviews: Galinsky, Gnomon 74 (2002) 685-687. Jamarťs article offers a probing parallel study of Vergil's Camilla and Statius' Parthenopaeus. McGill provides instances of author who sought to rework and reinterpret Aeneid themes. Som approached the epic as poetry capable of being made anew, recasting language and content with varying degrees of fidelity to the original. Pagán argues that both anachronistic ecphrases enabled the poet and historian to deal with "moments of extreme cultural and political crisis"(58). Pattimo's study of satiric degradation highlight Petronius' pilot and Vergil's Palinurus. The Watson volume of Martial's epigrams attaches particular interest to Epigram 8.5 (number 15, in their collection). Panoussi targets sinister contrast between Lucan 3 and Aeneid 6 (a case of deforestation to fuel the funeral pyre of Misenus) and the sea battle at Actium in Aeneid 8

who sought to rework and reinterpret Aeneid themes. Som
approached the epic as poetry capable of being made anew, recasting
language and content with varying degrees of fidelity to the original.
Pagán argues that both anachronistic ecphrases enabled the poet and
historian to deal with "moments of extreme cultural and political
crisis"(58). Pattimo's study of satiric degradation highlight
Petronius' pilot and Vergil's Palinurus. The Watson volume of
Martial's epigrams attaches particular interest to Epigram 8.5
(number 15, in their collection). Panoussi targets sinister contrast
between Lucan 3 and Aeneid 6 (a case of deforestation to fuel the
funeral pyre of Misenus) and the sea battle at Actium in Aeneid 8.

title: The Augustan Aeneis: Virgil Enlightened?
publication_block: Translation and Literature 1 1 (2002) 237-269. [Dryden] Ortiz's article treats the terrifying figure of Oroonoko, a fictional West African prince sold into slavery who featured in Aphra Behn's novella in 1688. Behn's story was reworked in 1695 in Thomas Southerne's tragedy, and most recently in Biyi Bandele's 1999 version. Behn's novella has been remodelled into drama, poetry, and even abolitionist propaganda. Ortiz realizes that Oroonoko: Or the Royal Slave, is a masterpiece of prose fiction with Vergilian traits

title: Frank Wittchow, "Vater und Onkel: Julius Caesar und das finale des Aeneis." Gymnasium 1 12 (
publication_block: 2005) 45-69. Smolenaars senses the evolution of Venus from love-goddess to Venus Genetrix, and the dynastic mythology of Augustus, in the encounter of Venus and Vulcan in Aeneid 8.370ff. The light-hearted scene activates the double identity of Venus, love-goddess and mother. The humor is irreverent and Alexandrian. Noonan argues that the ending of the epic is "part of the discourse of late Republican politics" (p. 43), and that contemporary civic standards are implied in the climactic battles in Latium (p. 34)

title: Philip Thibodeau, "Can Vergil Cry? Epicu 1.24," CJ 98 (
publication_block: 2003) 243-256. Clark shows that Vergil's poetry pervades Ho and thematically in a manner designed to pioneers a new breakthrough-even as Hor poetical and political levels. Horace reworks V the aftermath of Prometheus and his allusion to the civil wars in Eclogue 4. Deremetz studies the Sibyl in Vergil, Ovid, and Silius Italicus. Foulon engages with the Vergilian Sibyl in Propertius and Tibullus. Farrell argues that the ille ego 'false preface' to the epic was echoed by Ovid in his own career statement, which moves from an earlier ludic phase to rueful retrospective from exile. Galinsky detects verbal affinities between Horace's Cleopatra and Vergil's Dido and argues for creative interchange. Horace's Cleopatra in Odes 1.37 is one of many models for tragic Dido. Gosling discovers intratextual echoes between Vergil and Ovid. Ovid uses verbal allusion, rhetorical colouring and metrical emphasis to highlight his characterisation of Mezentius, redirecting the story to highlight Mezentius' brutality. Paul Miller engages with Dido's letter to Aeneas in Ovid's Heroides suggesting that Dido's voice opens up a dialogue with the monologic tendency of the epic which destabilises its version of events. John Miller suggests that Vergil's presentation of Actian Apollo reverberates in Propertius 2.4 which reveals knowledge of Aeneid 8 and Aeneid 1. Propertius 4.6 in Miller's assessment stresses Callimachean connections of Apollo's Delian origin, and provides a different poetic slant on Apollo's manifestation

title: J. Trappes-Lomax, "Hiatus in Vergil and in Horace's Odes." PCPS 50 (
publication_block: 2004) 141-158. Harrison notices that the word imago (= simile) in Vergil, Ovid and the Flavian poets is occasionally unearthed within, or in the immediate context of , an epic simile. The device is an aid to metaphorical effects: for intertextual cross-referencing to similes in previous epics, for intratextual purposes to link the relevant simile with previous material in the same epic, and for poetically ambiguous means to exploit the wide semantic range involved in the usage of imago. Alexander G. McKay 79

title: Roger Rees, ed., Romane Memento: Vergil in the Fourth Century
reviews: McKay, NECJ 32 (2005) 285-287. Dominik studies Statius' epilogue to the Thebaid 12.810-19 in a different light. Critics have generally read the lines as confession of poetic inferiority to Vergil; Dominik argues that the verses are meant to encourage readers to respect the intellectual merits of the epic and its role within a larger tradition. Feeney treats Statius' undernourished Achilleid from the standpoint of the sexual ambiguity of Achilles, the legacy from Ovidian and Vergilian epic, and the poet's concern to establish a distinctive creative difference between Thebaid and Achilleid. Quintus of Smyrna's 4th century capo lavoro soars in a scholarly revival. McGill provides the first systematic study of mythological and secular centos. Morzadec explores what 80 Annual Bibliography inspired Statius to plant imperial panegyric in the visionary speech (IV. 3.123-163)

authors: David Quint, "The Virgilian Coordinates of Paradise Lost." REPRESENTING 177-197
title: Joy Connolly deals with current work on the republican tradition i literature and political thought. She focuses on translations of Verg by the 17th century theorist James Harrington in a study of reception (114-126) of Vergil's pastorals and epic. David Quint assesses Milton's dark reworking of what appears to be positive politica material and reveals the negativity of the Vergilian original: the statesman simile in Book I "hints at the violence built into the settlement that Augustus imposed on the Roman political world" (p. 1 80), and the City of Pandemonium exposes the dark, hellish side of Vergil's Carthage and Rome. The fallen Satan and Adam resemble Aeneas after his shipwreck, starting afresh from the bottom

authors: Mike Pincombe, "Gloomy Orion: Eliot, Marlowe, Virgil." N&Q 50 (
title: ) 329-330. Johnson reviews Lowell's "Falling Asleep over the Aeneid." to discover and assess Lowell's ambivalence towards the epic and military imperialism in general. His vehicle is the 'exquisite ambiguity' of the funeral of Pallas. The fascistic color, sexual imagery, and ties with Lowell's own family losses in the Civil War are duly explored. The high-tide of New Criticism and foreshadowings of "Harvard-School" Vergilian scholarship are intriguingly displayed by Ralph Johnson. Lowrie's essay provides an exercise in Vergilian reception through Maurice Blanchot's thoughts on Hermann Broch's The Death of Virgil which he embodies in a short story, "The Moment of My Death." (Blanchot's reversal lays emphasis neither on self-sacrifice nor on sparing the epic at Vergil's death; the man survives and the written work is lost.)

title: Christopher Lehman, "Offend the Gods and Love Obey." Vergilius 50 (2004) 108-130. Colette traces Advent and Christmas sequences, and tropes and chanted breviary prayers, as testimony to the Sibyl as prophetess. The reliance of the works on a single monody suggests that they were very ancient. Fitzgerald (199-210) offers an analysis of Berlioz's Les Troyens and highlights female dominance in the action There is a large scale expansion of Cassandra's role in Part I as counterpart to Dido's premier role at Carthage. The composer chose to stress betrayal and destruction rather than triumph: the opera literally transforms the epic. Isabelle His traces the Sibyl in music that highlights Christ's birth, the Passion and the Resurrection. Twelve 16th century versions exhibit individual, iconographie model of the Sibyl. Maurice Ohana's three 20th century compositions include the Sibyl

title: Quali codici d'amore nella decima egloga di Virgilio? L'eloquio elegiaco contestualizzato nel Bueolicon liber
publication_block: Giornate filologiche "F. Della Corte: III (Genova 2003) 31-62. Macdonald studies the two songs in Eclogue 8 and detects a concentric arrangement of allusions to Theocritus' Idylls. Vergil uses the structure of the spell from Idyll 2 in both songs of Eclogue 8, but the second follows Simaetha's spell more closely. The contest songs create tension between internal and external imitation. Geymonat studies the vocatives at the start and finish of the Eclogue-Book. Shepherd and goats provide two extremes, both associated closely with the poet, and accenting the performance. Kraggerud accepts Heyne' s (te) in Eclogue 10.44 as inescapable in the context of Gallus' outburst: nunc insanus amor dum te martis in armis

title: This revelation grinds the heart in sorrow and yet gives hope in darkness
publication_block: (166) Konstan rejects the assumption that dementia had negative connotations during the 1st century BC and later. The traditional meaning is incorporated into the clupeus virtutis presented to Augustus. Lobe offers a perceptive study of the meaning and function of body-language around Roman epic. Alden Smith examines the implications of the Augustan transition from rhetoric to images to extol imperial power. Vergil is part of the contemporary process giving primacy to imagery over speech in the sensational epic adventures of Aeneas. Close reading of crucial episodes reveal that Aeneas repeatedly acts on what he see rather than what he hears. Suerbaum searches for bucolic elements in the Aeneid. Bucolic motifs far outweigh the factor of language. Shepherds appear not as singers but as fighters (Book 7); the wonderland of Arcadia and Book 8 is a close reflection. The major link is the subjective voice, the voice of compassion
raw_text: J. Whitehorne, "Reconciling East and West AUMLA 103 (2005) 1-14. Edgeworth/Stem explores Vergilian silence on points of interpretive value for the reader: Danaids in Tartarus? Return of Turnus' corpse? The silence reinforces the ambivalent ending of the epic. Danaids on the balteus of Pallas compound pietas and nefas. The counterweight of Aeneas' moral failure in killing Turnus lies in the relief that the killing secures Rome's future. publication_block: 2005). Horsfall provides a detailed commentary on Aeneid 3, called "The Dullest Book" in earlier days. The pages shimmer with fresh insights and a myriad of details. Students and scholars will welcome this first detailed commentary, with text, translation, and three indices. Dehon detects winter as the season for the African episode. title: Mezentius the Epicurean
publication_block: TAPA 135 (2005) 403-31. Kronenberg argues that Mezentius, surpassing Dido, evokes Epicurean philosophy in a manner that allows for an Epicurean reading of the poem's action. The Epicurean element in Mezentius helps to explain his surprising transformation from a symbol of impietas to pietas in Books 7-10, "as well as the reverse transformation of pius Aeneas into an impious Giant figure" (403). "Aeneas assumes the role of impious monster just as Mezentius is transformed into a pious philosopher." (425). Kronenberg offers a disturbing essay. However, Vergil's characters so often intertwine philosophic allusions with other sorts of literary models and codes that Epicurean readings of Dido and Mezentius cannot be proved "comprehensively wrong." 150 Alexander G. Mckay

title: Reading Virgil and his Texts: Studies in Intertextuality, Ann Arbor, MI
reviews: Damien Nelis, REA 107 (2005)819-820. Bernays is concerned with arithmetic exactitude in poetry and argues that Vergil followed the realm of absolute figures and divisible ratios in the Bucolics and the Aeneid (19-25); however, structural aspects require some drastic measures with the texts of Eclogues 2, 8, 9 and 10. McGill probes Epigrammata Bobiensia 46, 47 and 64, Latin versions of Greek epigrams on teachers. He argues for equivalent uses of the Iliad and Aeneid as central school texts in Greek and Latin grammatical schools

McDonald's collection of pastoral verses includes "Eclogue," an imitation of Vergil in the most emotionally engaged of the neoclassical pieces. Two voices debate the contemporary "troubles" as they traverse the Castlereagh Hills above Belfast; one is about to leave the city again. Tsao's novel is a modern version of the Aeneas and Dido tragic romance, beginning in
publication_block: 1959 in the Soviet Union and continuing into Communist China. Vasily (Zhao Huaxing, Aeneas) has been forced to leave his beloved Sophia in Russia where he cannot return nor can he convey her to China as the rift between the nations broadens. Vasily senses a parallel between Aeneas' determination to fulfill his destiny and his own Annual Bibliobraphy 161 dedication to his Party

title: 2004. Hesitant Heroes: Private Inhibition, Cultural Crisis. Ithaca and
reviews: P. Michelakis, CR 57 (2007) 133- 4. Hallett examines affinities between Sulpicia's poems and, among other topics, Vergil's treatment of Dido. Nadeau argues that Vergil establishes the harpy Celaeno as a trope for Sextus Pompeius. Solin shows that inscriptional testimony allows us to conclude that the river name Liris could also be used as a personal name (cf. Aen. 1 1.670). Ziolkowski explores the theme of heroic hesitation and its correlation with civic crisis through a discussion of the character of Aeneas and of a number of other heroes from antiquity to modernist times. publication_block: Promethe 84. Fréchet discusses Aen. 6.635-676. Knox suggests that the first book of Tibullus may have been published in 29 B.C. rather than in the mid-20s. This opens the door to a better contextualized examination of pastoral motifs in Tibullus and his relationship to Vergil's Eclogues. Lütkemeyer discusses Ovid's intertextual allusion to the first Eclogue. Pucci suggests that "The transgression Vergil and Horace practice in 1.3 is poetic composition itself." Putnam contains a chapter on Vergil

title: Eine Anspielung auf die Aeneas-Sage in HorazEpode 16?
publication_block: JAC 22: 131-9. Asztalos defends the transmitted reading "pluma" at Horace, carm. 4.10 on the ground that it alludes to Cycnus at Aen. 10.189-93 and, through this Vergilian filter, to carm. 2.20. title: Shades of Virgil: Seneca's Troades
publication_block: MD 61: 191-210. Aiello notes allusions to Vergil and Ovid; Bernstein to Aen. 12.435-40; Bruggisser to Aen. 9.735-7. Ferenczi discusses Valerius Flaccus' use of the Aeneid. Freyburger-Galland shows that Dio Cassius 75.10.2 translates Aen. 11.371-3, and asserts that this passage is Dio's only reference to Vergil. Fucecchi (2007) points to Vergil's influence on Statius, Th. 12.105f., and (2008a) argues that Valerius Flaccus' Amazon at 5.614 is modeled on Camilla in the Aeneid. Gamberale demonstrates that Jerome, epist. 22 alludes to Aen. 3.173 and 10.642. Gärtner includes discussion of the influence of Vergil on Valerius Flaccus. Gioseffi argues that Martial 14.58, followed then by Juvenal 9.102 and Alcuin, carm. 32, alludes to Eel. 2.69; and discusses the reception of Geo. 4.489. Guillaumin notes citation of Aen. 7.234-8 by the gromaticus Siculus Flaccus. Horsfall demonstrates that in at least six instances the text of Lycophron's Alexandra is given point if considered as a variation upon Vergil's Latin. Murgatroyd shows that Valerius alludes to Ovid and to Aen. 8.193-7; and Passarella that Ambrosius, Par. 7.35 alludes to Geo. 4.485-93. Petringa remarks on the influence of Vergil on Cyprian's poetry. Pisano observes Vergilian influence on Apuleius' story of Cupid and Psyche. Santini discusses Aen. 7 as a model for Pun. 13.115-37. Squire notices that allusion to Vergil is made in an inscribed epigra Sperlonga (AE 1967.85)
